title: Microfluidic Channels Wax Priming
author: Jorge Fernández
license_note: Confirmed via CrossRef DOI metadata: dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.8hmht46

steps.instruction: Set the hot plate to 105ºC or at least warm enough to melt the wax. Cut a small amount from one of the wax pencils and place it on a Petri dish. Set the Petri dish on the hot plate and wait for the wax to melt.
Cut to their appropiate size the nitrocellulose pieces to prime with the designed microfluidic path.

Once the wax is perfectly melted, take the plastic stamp and wet their sealing ends by immersion for 1-2 seconds in the molten wax. Lift the stamp and observe that the stamping surface is fully covered in wax.

As fast as possible place the stamp against the membrane, in order to release the wax on the surface of the nitrocellulose membrane. Apply mild pressure for 2-5 seconds, and lift the stamp. It is possible that the membrane goes up wtith the stamp. In that case, touch softly the outer region of the sealed membrane in order to detacht it from the stamp.

Cover the nitrocellulose strip with plastified paper that protects the membrane (one sheet on top, another underneath). Place the sandwich on the hot place with a Petri dish on top (this is just to apply soft pressure, any temperature-resistant flat object is valid here). Allow the strip to warm for 5 minutes. This will allow the wax to penetrate all through the strip.

Remove the strip from the hot plate and let it cool down again. You should see the shape of the channel wax primed on the membrane. Ussually it is vissible a thin "white shadow" near the border of the wax imprimation, that represents the limit of the primed channel. If the channel is not wide enough, or it is not uniform, consider repeating the procedure.
